# ANSWERS AND EXPLANATIONS FOR PRACTICE EXAM 3
## Scoring the Short-Answer Case History Questions

Short-answer responses are scored on a range from 0 to 2. First, this section presents the criteria for scoring short-answer responses to the PLT case histories. Then it shows how three different levels of short answers to the same question might be scored as a 2, a 1, or a 0 based on the scoring criteria that follow.
## Criteria for Scoring
For a score of 2, the response must
■ show the writer has a thorough understanding of the parts of the case history that relate to the question.
■ appropriately address all components of the question.
■ provide a strong explanation (if requested) and support it with relevant evidence.
■ provide evidence that the writer possesses strong knowledge of pedagogical concepts, facts, theories, methodologies, or procedures relevant to the question.
For a score of 1, the response must
■ show the writer has a basic understanding of the parts of the case history that relate to the question.
■ appropriately address one component of the question.
■ provide a weak explanation (if requested) but still be supported with relevant evidence.
■ provide evidence that the writer possesses some knowledge of pedagogical concepts, facts, theories, methodologies, or procedures relevant to the question.
For a score of 0, the response must
■ show the writer misunderstood the parts of the case history that relate to the question.
■ fail to address the question.
■ not be supported by relevant evidence.
■ provide little evidence that the writer possesses some knowledge of pedagogical concepts, facts, theories, methodologies, or procedures relevant to the question.
These sample responses are from Chapter 10, Case History 3.1, Mark Roberts. Read the case history to understand how we have used the criteria to score the three responses to the question.
## Sample Short-Answer Question

From what you know about Mark's language and social interaction skills from the classroom observation notes, where does he stand developmentally compared to peers his age? Are his skills and abilities developmentally appropriate? Explain your answer, basing your response on theories of language and social development.
## Sample of a Short Answer That Would Receive a 2

Developmentally, Mark's language skills appear to be slightly below the level of his four-year-old peers. From the observation we know that he does know the names of familiar animals and some colors, and he engages in pretend play and make-believe. However, he does not verbalize very much when he is engaged in activities and has not mastered the vocabulary to identify many common objects in picture books that most four-year-olds would be able to name. Some of his speech is not intelligible, and he exhibits some articulation problems. Socially, he appears to be developing confidence in sharing interactions with his peers through social play. Although play does not necessarily develop sequentially from parallel to social to cooperative play, typically preschoolers participate in all three types of play. From the observation notes, we have evidence that he is playing alongside peers (parallel play) and playing socially (cooperative). Further observation would be needed to determine to what extent he is also playing cooperatively with others (taking on complementary roles and shared purposes). We see him taking directions from John, but not cooperating in the sense that he contributes to and helps form the purposes of the play and roles. He does not appear to be an equal partner in social play and interactions, but rather a quiet follower.
Rationale: This is a sample of a level 2 response because it meets all of the following criteria.

■ By discussing specific details from the class observation and incorporating them into the response, this writer shows a thorough understanding of the parts of the case history that relate to the question.

- There are several components to the question: The writer is asked to address how Mark's development compares to his peers, whether his skills are developmentally appropriate, and pertinent theories of language and social development. All components of the question have been fully addressed.
■ The writer explains the answer and discusses the relevant evidence the passage offers to support the explanation.
■ The use of concepts and facts about development and play show strong knowledge of theories and methods.
## Sample of a Short Answer That Would Receive a 1

I would say Mark is developmentally delayed because he lacks confidence, based on his interactions with his sisters at home. They probably talk for him a lot, which has caused him not to talk for himself. He basically waits for others to tell him what to do. His skills are not developmentally appropriate because usually four-year-olds talk more and play and interact more openly with peers. He is following his friend around a lot. Although he talks some, his articulation should be assessed. Mark is in the egocentric stage of development. Maybe that's why he is quiet: He's focused on himself more than others.
Rationale: This is a sample of a level 1 response because it meets the following criteria.
- The response includes discussion of notes from the observation, but the level of understanding of Mark's development is basic at best. The response lacks clear insight.
■ The writer does respond to the first part of the question by answering that Mark is delayed developmentally.
- The writer's explanation is weak, but is somewhat supported by the theory of egocentrism.

■ Mentioning concepts such as articulation and egocentrism shows that the writer possesses some knowledge of pedagogical concepts, facts, theories, methodologies, or procedures relevant to the question.
## Sample Short Answer That Would Receive a 0

Mark's mother shouldn't let his sisters pick on him. When I was young, I was the youngest boy in the family. I had two older brothers. They always picked on me and I think that's probably why I decided to become a teacher. I want to be able to help students like Mark develop into confident, smart people who have a lot to offer the world. I think it's wrong to have another teacher follow Mark around and watch everything he's doing. Doesn't he have any privacy rights? The poor kid is getting picked on at home, and then when he comes to school he gets followed around and told what to do. Mark should be taken out of that school and put into a better one.
Rationale: This is a sample of a level zero response for the following reasons.
- The writer seems to have misunderstood the whole purpose of the observation. Instead of seeing the observation as a way to assess the student's needs, the writer took it as a sort of punishment.
■ The writer fails to answer any part of the question with any real insight about child development or pedagogy.
- No evidence from the case history is brought into the response to support the writer's perspective.
■ The writer brings in no theories or pedagogical concepts to show knowledge of how they might apply to this case history.
## Answers and Explanations
## PLT Case History 3.1 Mark Roberts

1. Developmentally, Mark's language skills appear to be slightly below the level of his four-year-old peers. From the observation we know that he does know the names of familiar animals and some colors, and he engages in pretend play and make-believe. However, he does not verbalize very much when he is engaged in activities, and has not mastered the vocabulary to identify many common objects in picture books that most four-year-olds would be able to name. Some of his speech is not intelligible, and he exhibits some articulation problems. Socially, he appears to be developing confidence in sharing interactions with his peers through social play. Although play does not necessarily develop sequentially from parallel to social to cooperative play, typically preschoolers participate in all three types of play. From the observation notes, we have evidence that he is playing alongside peers (parallel play) and playing socially (cooperative). Further observation would be needed to determine to what extent he is also playing cooperatively with others (taking on complementary roles and shared purposes). We see him taking directions from John, but not cooperating in the sense that he contributes to and helps form the purposes of the play and roles. He does not appear to be an equal partner in social play and interactions, but rather a quiet follower.

2. One factor that might contribute to Mark's rate of language acquisition is his gender. Mrs. Roberts has four older girls. She should be wary of comparing their language development with Mark's because he is a different gender. Whether it is due to nature or nurture, most girls typically talk earlier than most boys. Also, most late-talkers are young boys. Mrs. Salisbury should recommend Mark's language development be observed and documented to determine if there is a developmental delay. If there is a noted delay, the parent, school's speech and language specialist, and the child's physician should be informed so that follow-up assessments may be arranged. Another factor that might contribute to the rate of language acquisition is medical concerns. Temporary medical problems, such as otitis media, when left unattended, can cause language delays. Also, more serious congenital conditions, such as neurological problems, could cause delays. Mrs. Salisbury should look for and remind Mrs. Roberts to look for symptoms of ear infections, such as earaches, fever, loss of hearing ability, and unclear speech. If symptoms appear, the parents should notify the child's physician for follow-up diagnosis, care, and assessment of hearing loss and potential language delay.

3. First, early childhood teachers and parents of young children should demonstrate and model language and socialization skills by using vocabulary-rich language to talk about the world with children. Children will attempt to do what they see and hear others do. For example, reading aloud with children will cause them to independently pick up books and mimic the kind of language found in books, thereby expanding their language growth. Modeling functional uses of language and print for various purposes will cause children to learn the practical uses of language. In Mark's case, explicit modeling of appropriate social interactions, such as how Mark could ask other children if he could join their play, might be beneficial. Initial adult involvement in play interactions among peers would provide opportunities for Mark to witness appropriate ways to interact with his peers. Second, language acquisition is a process that occurs through social interactions among children and supportive adults. Therefore, adults must provide opportunities for children to play and work together in literacy-rich environments. In Mark's case, because he is the youngest child and the only boy sibling, the teacher might recommend inviting John and other peers over for play dates with Mark. Children learn by not only watching and listening, but by practicing, copying, and experiencing the social acts and language that occur around them. Therefore, teachers and parents should surround children with a language- and print-rich environment that involves children in interaction with functional print and language through play. Children will act as coaches for each other's learning.
## PLT Case History 3.2 Mr. Mo and Miss Danequa

1. According to the schedule, children participate in learning centers with the option of playing in the classroom or the gymnasium during the arrival and departure times. Mr. Mo and Miss Danequa might manage the activities by providing hands-on learning material in several learning centers. There should be a variety of learning centers available so children can self-select an area in which they are interested. The informal learning atmosphere centers would be ideal for the arrival and departure hours because children can enter and leave without disrupting others. Also, the teachers would be available to walk away from the centers when necessary to talk with parents and help the children transition. Activities in the centers should involve self-discovery and/or practice of previously mastered skills so children can participate with minimal assistance as they construct new understandings.

The role of the teachers during the arrival time is to meet and greet the children and then show and tell them what centers are available that particular day. Before allowing the children to leave the entry area of the classroom, the teacher should be sure to help the child get his or her possessions organized in the proper place, ask if there are any notes or special messages from home, and generally converse casually with the child and parent. When ready to escort the child into the centers areas, the teacher should state any expectations, then help the child get involved in the activity and/or with his or her peers. In the centers, the teachers' roles might include facilitating dramatic play, promoting discussion among peers, answering questions, and encouraging and modeling appropriate language and social skills. The teacher might read with the children or otherwise provide impromptu, individual attention. Additionally, the teacher must ensure the children are safe, so there should always be at least one supervising teacher in each room. The same is true for departure time.

2. To help children construct meanings about growing up through a literacy center, the teacher could provide a wide array of picture books about the theme. If a computer is available, the teacher could also find an appropriate website to share, along with other available resources. For example, books about infants and toddlers and their special physical needs (e.g., diapering, bottle feeding, sleeping in cribs, being walked in strollers, etc.) could prompt discussions of babies' dependency on others to take care of them physically because they cannot take care of themselves. After viewing the books and talking about the theme, the children could be encouraged to draw what they learned about how they changed as they grew out of infancy. Teachers could help them write words, a sentence, or even a whole story about the process of growing up.

In a science center, the teacher could provide pictures of the stages of a butterfly's metamorphosis for the children to arrange. They could match their results by looking up the stages in a book or in another appropriate resource such as a website. A science center could also house a collection of fruits, vegetables, seeds, seedlings, saplings, and plants that show growth and change. Children could plant seeds and, over time, observe and record a plant's growth by measuring and recording its height and width.

3. Overall, the schedule takes into consideration the fact that young children need a variety of activities that move from quiet, restful times to more active, physical times. There are some teacher-lead times (e.g., read-alouds) interspersed among the hands-on activities (e.g., learning centers and physical exercise). There are times in the schedule where the teacher can facilitate the children's development of gross motor skills, fine motor skills, language, cognition, social skills, and self-help skills. The time slots on the schedule appeal to a young child's short attention span, also. For the most part, the children are not expected to sit in one area for a long time. However, it is unclear what the children are expected to do during the "quiet work" hour at the end of the day. Developmentally, the children are not ready to sit and work on something for a full hour. This time might be replaced with 20 minutes of sharing books with a friend, followed by 20 minutes of song and dance, and then 20 minutes of drawing and coloring. The shortened time slots and change of activities from quiet to active and back to quiet might be more appropriate for three- and four-year-olds.
## PLT Case History 3.3 Ms. Gentry

1. Carrie is a good teacher. She cares about her students. She's a good person. That's why I was surprised to see her confronting her student in such a way to make him feel so helpless and embarrassed. Maybe she was just having a bad day? I worry that she's lost her students' trust, though. What his mother wrote in the note wasn't his fault. Everyone forgets things sometimes, including children . . . and mothers and teachers! What Carrie has to be careful of now is not to lose the respect and trust of the rest of her students and the other adults who were in the room at the time. I work hard to build trust and respect among my colleagues and my students. I don't think it's right to reprimand someone, first of all, for something that isn't his or her fault; secondly, by yelling at him or her; and thirdly, in front of others in a way that embarrasses the child in front of peers. We all just need to communicate in a kind, understanding way and then try to work on a solution together.

2. Ms. Gentry does seem troubled by the fact that Carrie belittled the boy in front of others. She understands the importance of developing a high self-esteem from an early age. We don't know the boy, or how sensitive or emotionally mature he is. Ms. Gentry may be concerned that the boy's self-esteem will be lowered based on this interaction with his teacher. Self-esteem refers to how we value ourselves. Self-esteem patterns start early in life. It is developed through our successes, interactions with others, self-image, and feelings of acceptance and love. Also, teachers should model compassion and understanding for others so students will learn how to respond to others. If they see a teacher, who is seen as an authority, yelling at someone, they may mimic the reaction. It isn't clear whether the other children understood that the reason the boy was getting reprimanded wasn't even his fault. If they did, there is reason to believe they might learn to mistrust their teacher.

3. The best way to respond to the note would be to contact the parent directly and have a rational discussion of the situation. A face-to-face parentteacher conference might be best to attempt to avoid misinterpretations and miscommunications. Building a supportive community between home and school constructs an important foundation for helping children to develop confidence and trust in themselves and others. If the child has a history of forgetting his belongings here and there, the parent and teacher could discuss a plan to help him be less forgetful. Also, the teacher might recommend further observations be made to see if this is a recurring problem or just an occasional occurrence. The child should be part of the conversation so, over time, he learns how to be responsible about taking care of his things.
## PLT Case History 3.4 Alexandria

1. Most state governments mandate that when teachers and other school personnel suspect or witness child abuse they are required to report it to the state authorities. Alexandria's teacher and nurse must report this to the state and the school administrator or day-care owner immediately. Ms. Lorenzo most likely must return to her class. So Nurse Carolyn could make the call. Typically, states have a toll-free number for reporting abuse and methods for maintaining anonymity of the reporter.

2. Ms. Lorenzo should notify the school administrator or day-care owner of the situation and ask for his or her advice. This is not the kind of problem that anyone should solve alone, due to the public nature of schools. The situation should be discussed and carefully considered. The teacher may be advised to encourage the parent to continue to volunteer in the classroom and participate in parent meetings. Or some other solution may be devised. With the help of people trained and skilled in helping the abuser—whomever he or she turns out to be—there is hope.

3. Ms. Lorenzo should provide a responsive, predictable environment for Alexandria so she feels safe and cared for. Ms. Lorenzo can act as Alexandria's primary provider—a person who provides repeated and consistently warm responsiveness—so the child understands she can obtain comfort from adults through appropriate social relationships. Ms. Lorenzo should help Alexandria express herself and understand her range of emotions.
## Multiple-Choice Questions I

1. a. It is particularly helpful to take the time to record informal observations of children as they play and participate in activities throughout the day. The implications for doing this on a regular basis are profound because these mini-reports can potentially be used in creating instructional tasks that are developmentally appropriate for the child.

2. c. The National Association for the Education of Young Children's (NAEYC) guidelines for developmentally appropriate assessment include the principles that children should be assessed for what they can do independently as well as what they can do with help from other children or adults. The guidelines also state that assessment relies on observations of development and performance during authentic activities, and that multiple sources of relevant information should be relied upon.

3. a. The natural development and inquisitive nature of children lead the Reggio Emilia philosophy. The school, which focuses on preschool and primary education, originated in Italy; however, it has spread throughout the world. The approach is based on the principles that children should help control their learning as they learn through experiences; children must be encouraged to explore their environment and relationships with others; and children should be given endless ways to express themselves.

4. a. Vygotsky believed that learning occurs on a continuum. On one end of the continuum was the child's ability to solve problems on his or her own. On the other end was the child's ability to solve the problem with a maximum amount of adult help or peer collaborative assistance. He called this continuum the zone of proximal development, or ZPD. He believed children should work within this ZPD for optimum learning.

5. a. Invented by Wood and Middleton (not by Vygotsky, as is commonly assumed), the term scaffolding consists of involving children in joint problem solving with an adult or another, more knowledgeable peer. As the two collaborate toward the common goal, the child gains new understandings and is guided by the adult who points out the bridge between the child's prior knowledge and the new information.

6. a. Gender differences have been found to influence cognitive development. Boys are at greater risk for language disorders, reading and spelling difficulties, attention-concentration deficiencies, oral language problems, memory sequencing problems, directional confusion, and linguistic deficiencies. Boys can be anywhere from 6 to 18 months behind their female same-age peers.

7. b. Piaget, a developmental constructivist who thought development led learning, saw cognitive development as the result of maturation and experience. He proposed that children move through a series of developmental stages.

8. d. Constructivist-based classrooms provide a multitude of opportunities for meaningful discussions and explorations involving both teacher-child and child-child collaborative and cooperative learning. Activities related to the curriculum are designed to be useful and engaging to the children.

9. c. Maslow was a humanist who believed humans strive for the highest level of consciousness and wisdom they can reach. To achieve their highest levels, however, first their basic needs must be met. Maslow believed educators must respond to the potential all students have to reach self-actualization, the highest level of Maslow's hierarchy of needs.

10. d. NAEYC promotes a variety of resources for early childhood professional development. Their website (www.naeyc.org) offers links to resources and information on their annual convention, publications, and training sessions. Membership benefits include reduced rates on conference registrations, access to the association's network of affiliate groups, and subscription to their peer-reviewed professional journals.

11. a. Teachers can encourage the building of classroom communities that instill respect, responsibility, and compassion by modeling and expecting caring and fairness among all members of the classroom community. Rich classroom communities build a foundation for cognitive, emotional, and social growth and development.

12. d. Computers and other forms of technology should be placed in classrooms where teachers have been trained in integrating them successfully into the curriculum. Simply dumping computers into a classroom and expecting children to use them on their own can be highly inappropriate and a waste of funds. The effects of technology on learning should be closely monitored.
## Multiple-Choice Questions II

1. d. Direct instruction, commonly referred to as DI, is an instructional method that is characterized by explicit, systematic curriculum design; a prescribed script for the teacher to read and follow; ability grouping; accelerated student progress through a fast pace and efficiency; frequent assessment; and careful monitoring of embedded professional development.

2. a. The child is the center of Bronfenbrenner's ecological model of child development. The model proposes that a child interacts and is connected with his or her family, early education and care, health, and other community learning. Child development is influenced by what the child experiences in various settings. These experiences are called proximal processes and are considered by Bronfenbrenner to be essential in human development.
3. c. The ADA (Public Law 101-336) prohibits discrimination against people with disabilities. It was signed into law in 1990 by President George Bush.

4. d. Intrinsic motivation suggests students are motivated through their own accord, as opposed to being extrinsically motivated by some outside fuel or motivating factor. Examples of intrinsic motivation would be studying something just because you are interested in it, not because you want any recognition or reward for learning; doing something just because you feel it is the right thing to do; and creatively composing a poem to express your inner feelings.

5. a. Bloom's Taxonomy categorizes educational objectives into three domains. The affective domain relates to emotions and feelings; the psychomotor domain includes development in behavior and skills; and the cognitive domain involves the skills traditional education tends to focus on (e.g., knowledge, comprehension, application, analysis, synthesis, and evaluation).

6. b. The deficit model of teaching assumes children do not learn or do not understand something because they are lacking in some way. The deficit model contrasts the idea that a teacher's role is to facilitate understanding and learning by drawing out learners' tacit knowledge.

7. a. Service learning is a teaching method that integrates instruction and reflection with meaningful community service. The objective of this type of hands-on learning is to transform all involved—the community members, educators, and students. It offers participants opportunities to address the needs, concerns, and ambitions of their community.

8. b. Teachers must know and understand child development across various disciplines, not just in the cognitive realm. Being a teacher involves responding to a child emotionally, physically, cognitively, and socially. To most effectively support a child's overall development, teachers must be aware of the differences in children's development and how they influence their learning. Children develop at different rates. Being able to recognize where a child is developmentally will help the teacher know how to scaffold the child's learning across disciplines.

9. d. Teachers who model confidence in accepting challenges and solving problems can influence students' development of self-esteem. Self-esteem refers to how one values or feels about him- or herself, not others. We typically identify children as having high or low self-esteem. Interactions with others can influence one's self-concept, or how we think about ourselves.

10. a. When feeding an infant, positions do vary based on the age, physical size, movement patterns, and postural tone of the infant. Unless an infant is being bottle- or breast-fed, he or she should be positioned as upright as possible. Avoid causing the child's body to extend by applying hand pressure to the back of his or her head while feeding. Finally, use nipples with regular-sized holes to avoid causing the infant to choke by sucking too much liquid.

11. d. It is best to teach rudimentary communication behaviors by systematically manipulating and designing the environment to provide opportunities for the child to communicate his or her needs. For example, the teacher could display high-preference items the child typically desires. Ignoring, rewarding, and punishing the child are not positively effective methods for teaching communication behaviors.

12. b. One of the most important components of co-teaching is to schedule time to plan and evaluate the co-teaching experience. Co-teaching without planning what will be taught, who will do what, and what needs to be done to prepare for the experience can be highly ineffective. Evaluating the experience will encourage teachers to reflect on what is working and what should be adjusted. Through planning, the activities will be planned in detail. Most teachers do not find they need to practice or write out lesson plans for co-teaching. Finally, teaching with someone who has a different philosophy of teaching from your own can be enlightening. You might just learn something yourself!
## Praxis II Scoring

Evaluate how you did on this practice exam by scoring your short-answer responses and your multiple-choice answers. Bring your short-answer responses to a trusted friend to score so you can better see how you did. Reflect on the types of questions you missed most frequently.

The key to doing well on the Praxis II is to practice, read widely, and stay organized and focused. You have self-assessed your strengths and weaknesses and learned how to manage the various types of questions on the test. Enter the exam with confidence, knowing you are ready and equipped to do your very best!
